gist: Ed Yardeni argues the Fed will drop its easing bias at the June FOMC and may jump straight to a tightening bias, with an outlier call for an eventual rate hike, driven by firming labor data and an oil shock; yet he holds an 8,250 S&P 500 year-end target on 'fabulous earnings momentum,' advises an energy overweight as a geopolitical hedge, and flags the SpaceX/Anthropic/OpenAI IPO wave as potentially 'rigged for success' via rule changes forcing index and retail buying.

insights.claim: Yardeni expects the Fed to drop its easing bias at the June 17 FOMC statement, citing April FOMC minutes that read as hawkish (three dissenters wanted the easing bias dropped completely) despite Powell's dovish framing of them.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A confirmed removal of the easing bias would reprice front-end rates and pressure rate-cut bets; watch the June 17 statement and presser.
insights.quote: I think it's pretty clear that the easing bias will be dropped at the June, uh, in the June statement.

insights.claim: Yardeni thinks the Fed could skip a neutral bias and jump straight to a tightening bias at the June meeting, with the question being whether they sit on it into July or act on it.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A direct move to a tightening bias is more hawkish than markets price; a hawkish surprise risk for risk assets into late June.
insights.quote: I I think, uh, they could jump, uh, straight to a tightening, uh, bias.

insights.claim: Yardeni's outlier view is a likely Fed rate hike, probably a quarter point, that markets are not currently expecting, motivated partly by the Fed's wounded credibility from being late to tighten in 2021-early 2022.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: An out-of-consensus hike call; if it materializes it could trigger a buying-opportunity dip rather than a sustained selloff per Yardeni.
insights.quote: rate cuts are definitely off the table. Uh a tightening bias is very likely at the June meeting coming up. And then um uh a likely rate hike uh uh that that's our outlier view

insights.claim: Yardeni expects a July hike only if the Fed first moves incrementally, telegraphing the possibility of a hike through speeches before acting.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Watch Fed speakers this week and after June for hike-signaling language as the tell on July timing.
insights.quote: I think they would want to more incrementally uh to to to move more incrementally towards hiking. If that's their plan, which would includes starting to discuss the possibility of a hike in various speeches.

insights.claim: Yardeni expects Kevin Warsh, who has signaled he may be less communicative than Powell, to nonetheless hold a press conference at the June meeting laying out his view.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A Warsh-led FOMC implies a more hawkish reaction function; his communication style is a new variable for rate volatility.

insights.claim: Yardeni argues a Fed rate hike cannot resolve an Iran-driven oil/inflation shock or a bond selloff, but the Fed may tighten anyway out of concern about financial stability and speculative excesses if the market keeps melting up and SpaceX 'goes to the moon.'
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Frames a hike as a lean-against-bubbles move rather than an inflation cure; speculative froth itself becomes a tightening trigger.
insights.quote: I think the the the Fed has to be concerned about the potential of uh them being uh not restrictive enough when it comes to financial stability and speculative excesses.

insights.claim: Yardeni concedes the Fed may matter less than it thinks, noting that hindsight suggests hiking 3-6 months earlier in 2021-2022 would have made little difference given the pandemic-driven nature of that inflation.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A reminder to weight supply-side/geopolitical drivers over Fed policy when judging the inflation path.
insights.quote: maybe the Fed doesn't matter as much as they think they matter.

themes.theme: Inflation, oil shock and the dual mandate

insights.claim: Yardeni sees the dual-mandate risk balance shifting: employment-side risks are diminished while inflation-side risks are increased, leading him to conclude the Fed could issue a tightening bias in June.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A firming labor market removes the dovish offset, clearing the path for a hawkish Fed; jobs data surprises now cut hawkish.
insights.quote: the the risks are diminished on the employment side. The risks are increased on the on the inflation side.

insights.claim: Oil prices are up roughly 6-7% on the day as Iran refuses to exchange notes with the US on extending the Gaza-area ceasefire and threatens to shut the Strait of Hormuz, making the Fed more likely to treat the oil shock as persistent rather than transitory by FOMC time.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A persistent oil shock is the swing factor pushing the Fed hawkish; track Hormuz/ceasefire headlines into June 17.
insights.quote: the oil prices are up about 6 7%.

insights.claim: Exxon and Chevron have both warned that global oil inventories are running 'bone dry,' which Yardeni says could drive an oil price spike to $150.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A tight-inventory backdrop amplifies geopolitical oil upside; supports energy exposure and a higher-inflation tail.
insights.quote: Exxon and Chevron have been both warning that the inventories are running bone dry Uh around the world and that could lead to a spike in oil prices up to $150.

insights.claim: Yardeni warns the Fed could conclude it faces not just an energy problem but an energy shock with potential to spread into a broader list of prices, ahead of the CPI, PPI and employment reports that precede the June FOMC.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Watch whether the pre-FOMC CPI/PPI show oil bleeding into core; broadening price pressure is what converts a bias to a hike.
insights.quote: they've got not just an energy problem but an energy problem that has a potential to spread into a broader list of prices.

insights.claim: Yardeni expects May labor-market data to surprise to the upside, noting March and April were stronger than expected and all major labor indicators point to continued strength.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A hot May jobs report would reinforce the hawkish-Fed thesis; position for upside-surprise risk in payrolls.
insights.quote: labor market's kind of been going our way. It's we've been saying that it could surprise people to the upside

insights.claim: Yardeni maintains an 8,250 S&P 500 year-end target, framing the rally as an earnings-led 'melt-up' (his 'FEMO' / fabulous earnings momentum) rather than a valuation-driven 'FOMO' bubble.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: An above-market year-end target anchored to earnings, not multiples; the bull case survives a hawkish Fed but breaks on recession.
insights.quote: Still though hanging on to 8,250 S&P 500 by by year end.

insights.claim: Q1 earnings season is finishing around 19% growth, with double-digit growth rates being raised across the board all the way through Q4, driven heavily by AI and semiconductor companies beating expectations.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Concrete earnings-momentum evidence underpinning the melt-up; rising forward estimates, not multiple expansion, are doing the work.
insights.quote: 19% is the way we're uh Now, ending up uh the Q1 earning season.

insights.claim: S&P 500 forward earnings keep hitting record highs and will converge by year-end toward analysts' rising 2027 estimates, with information-technology 2026 and 2027 estimates both climbing.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: The time-weighted roll to 2027 numbers mechanically lifts forward EPS, supporting the index even if multiples hold.

insights.claim: Tech-sector forward earnings have been so strong that the tech forward P/E is now only modestly above the overall market, versus a prior tech forward P/E near 40 against a much lower market multiple.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Earnings catch-up has de-risked tech valuations relative to history, a key plank of the not-irrational-exuberance case.
insights.quote: the forward PE was was 40 for the technology sector and high but much lower for the rest of the market.

insights.claim: Yardeni says the recession scenario is the key risk that would 'clobber' the bull case on both P/E and E, collapsing the FEMO thesis and bringing the ~21x S&P 500 (23-24x tech) multiple down.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Defines the binary: no recession = melt-up continues; recession = bear market via simultaneous P/E and E hits. Monitor recession signals above all.
insights.quote: but bear markets is what you get when you have a recession. Because you get clobbered on both PE and E.

insights.claim: Yardeni notes breadth is improving, with small-cap and mid-cap forward earnings breaking out to record highs after a prolonged 'coma,' and the equal-weighted S&P 500 also making a new record high.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Broadening earnings breadth supports rally durability beyond mega-cap tech; constructive for small/mid-cap exposure.

insights.claim: Yardeni reads bull-bear sentiment as neutral, which he interprets as leaving more upside in the bull run rather than signaling a near-term top.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Lack of euphoria is a contrarian positive; a stretched bull-bear ratio at highs would be the warning he's not yet seeing.
insights.quote: not not too many bulls, not too many bears. That suggests that there's more upside from a sentiment perspective

insights.claim: The S&P 500 sits about 11% above its 200-day moving average, with technology, materials, energy, services and some industrials the most extended areas.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Flags near-term stretch and pullback vulnerability in the most-extended sectors despite the bullish secular call.
insights.quote: the market is a bit extended here at 11% for the S&P 500 above its 200-day.

insights.claim: Yardeni is sticking with an energy-sector overweight as a hedge against current geopolitical risks, viewing it as a reasonable place to be given the Iran/oil backdrop.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Direct actionable tilt: own energy as a geopolitical/oil hedge while the conflict persists.
insights.quote: we're we've kind of stuck with our energy overweight and kind of thinking about that's not a bad place to be kind of on a hedging basis for the geopolitical issues

insights.claim: Yardeni advises near-term caution, expecting June to get choppy as markets juggle oil prices, the FOMC, and the SpaceX IPO.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Tactical caution flag for June despite the bullish year-end target; expect volatility around these three catalysts.
insights.quote: I'm inclined to think things could get a little choppy here.

insights.claim: Yardeni favors 'stay-home' US assets over 'go-global' for now, arguing global/emerging-markets outperformance won't work until the war is over and oil falls, and that the ex-US trade is really just a South Korea and Taiwan play.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Underweight ex-US until war/oil resolve; treat EM ETFs like EMXC as concentrated Taiwan/Korea tech bets, not broad EM.
insights.quote: until the war's over at the a stay home is going to outperform for a while for a while longer.

insights.claim: Yardeni argues emerging markets will not appreciate a Fed moving toward a tightening bias or an actual hike, adding a second-order headwind to ex-US equities.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A hawkish-Fed scenario compounds the case against EM; links his rate call to his stay-home positioning.
insights.quote: Emerging markets are not going to uh enjoy seeing the Fed move towards a tightening bias, and they certainly aren't going to appreciate a Fed rate hike.

insights.claim: Yardeni believes the market can absorb a Fed hike, expecting any dip to be another buying opportunity as investors conclude a quarter or 50 bps makes little difference against a resilient economy.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Reconciles his hawkish-Fed and 8,250 bull calls: treat a hike-driven selloff as a dip to buy, not a regime change.
insights.quote: it could present another one of these uh buying opportunities uh because people will say what difference does a quarter or 50 basis points make and the economy will continue to to show its resilience.

insights.claim: Yardeni flags Middle East data centers and Persian Gulf undersea telecom cables as physically vulnerable to the conflict, reinforcing a US-asset premium while the war continues.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Geopolitical infrastructure risk argues for a US-located premium in AI/data-center exposure.
insights.quote: Data centers in the Middle East look somewhat vulnerable. So do the uh telecom cables under the strait running in the Persian Gulf look vulnerable.

insights.claim: Yardeni estimates SpaceX, Anthropic and OpenAI could carry a combined public market cap of $4-5 trillion once trading, while actually raising only about $250-300 billion across the three.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Sizes a massive new supply of mega-cap AI/space equity; the gap between market cap and cash raised shapes the liquidity question.
insights.quote: the market cap could be somewhere between $4 and $5 trillion.

insights.claim: Yardeni suggests the IPOs may be 'rigged for success': exchanges changed rules to ease public trading and index inclusion, forcing index funds to buy, while brokers cold-call retail (including Yardeni himself) to participate amid a small SpaceX float.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Forced index buying plus a small float can drive sharp initial upside; flags structural demand but also rigged-pricing risk for late retail buyers.
insights.quote: are these things being rigged for success? I mean, certainly look looks that way.

insights.claim: The SpaceX IPO is scheduled for June 12, just five days before the June 17 FOMC statement, clustering two major catalysts in mid-June.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Concrete event calendar: position for a high-event-density mid-June window combining the IPO and a hawkish Fed.
insights.quote: SpaceX is scheduled for June 12th.

insights.claim: Yardeni warns the small SpaceX float and retail offer could see early gains met by daily retail selling, making post-IPO price action unpredictable.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Caution on chasing the IPO pop; retail distribution could cap or reverse initial upside.
insights.quote: Question is whether that that'll may be met by a daily selling by selling by by retail.

insights.claim: Yardeni views a 10-year Treasury yield range of roughly 4.25-4.75% (broadly 4-5%) as a normal, range-bound bond market consistent with a healthy economy, not the 'crashing' bond market headlines feared.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Reframes higher yields as normalization, not distress; supports staying invested in equities despite the rate level.
insights.quote: we kind of view 4 to 5% really 4 and 1/4 to 4 and 3/4% is kind of the the range-bound bond market that is consistent with with normal.

insights.claim: Yardeni notes the US is running roughly $2 trillion deficits (stated as '200 billion'), citing fiscal stimulus as a reason the economy has stayed resilient and bond yields are elevated.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Fiscal support is part of why the economy passes stress tests; also a structural floor under yields.
insights.quote: We are running 200 billion dollar deficits.

insights.claim: Yardeni's long-run framework sees a rotation into gold and bonds via rebalancing as the S&P 500 climbs toward 10,000 by the end of the decade and investors grow nervous and de-risk.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Rebalancing flows are a structural long-term bid for gold and bonds even in a bull-equity scenario.
insights.quote: they'll rebalance into bonds and they'll rebalance into gold.

insights.claim: Yardeni notes gold's recent driver has shifted: prior central-bank buying has given way to some central banks selling dollar and gold reserves to defend their currencies amid the war, and a re-escalation would likely push gold down rather than up.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Counterintuitive: war escalation may pressure gold via reserve selling; watch the 200-day and trendline support.
insights.quote: the war has caused some central banks to have to scramble to uh support their currency and they've been doing it by selling dollars uh reserves and and gold reserves.

insights.claim: Margin buying is at a record high, which Yardeni says does not itself cause a bear market but would make any future bear market more severe.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Record leverage is an amplifier, not a trigger; raises the downside tail if a recession or shock hits.
insights.quote: It's at a record high margin buying. The margin buying only tells you that the bear market could be more severe

insights.claim: Yardeni flags consensus long-term earnings growth expectations (LTEG) for the S&P 500 at about 22% per year over the next 3-5 years, a record ex-pandemic, which he calls unrealistic versus a more realistic 10-15%.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A sub-1 PEG ratio rests on over-optimistic LTEG; in a recession these expectations collapse fast, a hidden valuation risk.
insights.quote: long-term earnings growth expectations by analysts on a consensus basis is up to like 22% per year over the next 3 to 5 years

themes.theme: Structural economy: labor share and boomer wealth

insights.claim: Yardeni confirms labor's share of national income has fallen to a record-low 53.8%, down from 65% in 1978, the flip side of strong corporate margins driving FEMO.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Margin expansion at labor's expense is structurally bullish for earnings but a political/economic fault line to watch.
insights.quote: the labor mark labor share in uh national income is down. Uh in 1978 was 65% of GDP.

insights.claim: Yardeni argues the consumer economy's resilience is driven by baby-boomer demographics: ~$89 trillion of boomer net worth, retirees spending down while they still can, plus ~30% of unmarried young adults living at home and ~80% receiving parental financial support.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Boomer wealth transfer and spending explains the unusually resilient consumer; a structurally bullish demand backdrop independent of wages.
insights.quote: what you're not mentioning is the 89 trillion dollars of net worth of the baby boomers
